questions.prompt: Complete the sentence: "The concert starts at 8 p.m., ___"
questions.answer: so don't be late. (timetable)

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence: "I'm meeting Anna for coffee tomorrow — ___"
questions.answer: we arranged it last week. (arrangement)

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence: "Look at those clouds! ___"
questions.answer: It's going to rain. (evidence)

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence: "I'll have the chicken, please. ___"
questions.answer: (spontaneous decision at the moment)

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence: "She's going to study medicine. ___"
questions.answer: She decided last year. (intention)

questions.prompt: The train _____ (leave) at 7:30 tomorrow morning. Don't miss it. (timetable)
questions.answer: leaves — Use the simple present for timetables: 'The train leaves at 7:30.'

questions.prompt: I _____ (be / go) to the gym tonight. I booked a class last week. (arrangement)
questions.answer: 'm going — Use the present progressive for fixed arrangements: 'I'm going to the gym.'

questions.prompt: I think she _____ (do) really well in the exam. (general prediction)
questions.answer: will do — Use 'will' for general predictions: 'I think she will do really well.'

questions.prompt: He _____ (not come) to the party. He told me he has other plans. (intention)
questions.answer: isn't going to come — Use 'going to' for intentions already decided: 'He isn't going to come.'

questions.prompt: _____ they meeting tomorrow? Is it confirmed? (arrangement)
questions.answer: Are — Use the present progressive for fixed arrangements: 'Are they meeting tomorrow?'

questions.prompt: Find the mistake: "I going to see the doctor tomorrow morning."
questions.answer: 'Going to' needs the verb 'to be': 'I'm going to see the doctor tomorrow morning.'

questions.prompt: Find the mistake: "The train wills leave at half past seven."
questions.answer: 'Will' never takes -s: 'The train will leave at half past seven.'

questions.prompt: Find the mistake: "She isn't going to comes to the party tonight."
questions.answer: After 'going to', use the base verb: 'She isn't going to come to the party.'

questions.prompt: Find the mistake: "I will to call you when I arrive at the station."
questions.answer: After 'will', use the base verb with no 'to': 'I will call you.'

questions.prompt: Find the mistake: "We're meeting for lunch on Saturday — are you come?"
questions.answer: For arrangements using the present progressive, use the -ing form: 'are you coming?'
She's going to study medicine.
I will call you later.
The show starts at nine.
We're having lunch tomorrow.
It's not going to rain.
